result:
The first thing that comes to me is the silence. Not a true silence, of course. There’s the hum of the refrigerator, a distant, watery rush of a car on a wet street, the soft, almost imperceptible click of my own thoughts arranging themselves into these words. But the *feeling* is of silence—a wide, blank page silence.

I think about the weight of 1000 words. It’s not an essay. It’s not a story, unless you make it one. It’s a substance, a volume to be filled. Like being given a jar and told to go out and collect until it’s full. Do you cram it with pebbles? With fine, grey sand? With a single, perfect orchid and the air around it? The choosing is the task.

Lately, I’ve been fascinated by the concept of “middles.” We glorify beginnings and endings. The first page, the last line, the birth, the death, the hello, the goodbye. But the vast, unglamorous territory is the middle. The Tuesday afternoon. The second season of a show. The years of a marriage after the sparkle and before the stillness. The humble, sturdy conjunction “and then.” Life is almost entirely middle. It’s the long walk between the door of the house and the door of the car. It’s the chewing of the food, not the ordering or the paying. It is this sentence, right here, neither starting nor finishing the thought, but carrying it forward.

A memory surfaces, unbidden: my grandmother’s hands, kneading dough. I must have been six or seven, perched on a stool, watching. They were capable hands, marked with faint brown spots and raised blue veins like rivers on a map. There was no recipe. She knew by feel—the give of the flour and water and yeast under her palms, the sticky resistance becoming smooth and elastic. That was a middle, too. The in-between state of becoming bread. The waiting. The warm, yeasty smell that filled the kitchen was the scent of transformation in progress. She’s gone now, and I never learned to make her bread. I only remember the middle of the process, the alchemy of her moving hands.

This makes me think of bridges. Another kind of middle. Their sole purpose is to span a gap, to connect two points that would otherwise be separate. No one builds a house on a bridge. You don’t stop there. It is pure transition. And yet, some of the most beautiful human constructions are bridges. We paint them, we light them up at night, we sing about them burning down. We recognize their necessity, their elegance, even as we hurry across.

Perhaps we should learn to love our middles more. The messy desk, the half-read book, the project that is neither idea nor accomplishment. The quiet, ongoing work of maintaining a friendship, which lacks the drama of reunion or rupture. The body that is no longer young but not yet old, bearing the marks of living like a well-used tool.

Outside my window, the light is changing. It’s that long, golden hour of late afternoon, when everything seems dipped in honey. Shadows stretch out, lazy and long. This is the middle between the bustling day and the deep night. It is the most beautiful time, I think, precisely because it is a transition. It doesn’t last. It is all the more precious for its transience.

I wonder about you, reading this. You are in the middle of your day, your life, your own train of thought. You picked this up in a pause, between things. We are having a conversation in the middle of everything else. I am throwing these words into the jar, and you are sorting through them, taking some, leaving others.

There’s a pressure, always, to have a point. To tie it all together with a ribbon by word 1000. But maybe the point is the wandering. The permission to not conclude, but to simply *be* in the linguistic field for a while. Like a walk without a destination. You notice the crack in the pavement shaped like Italy. You hear a snatch of a argument from an open window. You feel the wind shift from west to east. The walk is not about getting somewhere; it is about the collection of sensations.

I am, I realize, building a cairn. Piling one observation on top of another, not to create a monument, but simply to say: *I passed this way*. These are the stones I noticed. They are smooth from the river of my attention. Some are plain, some have a flash of quartz. They hold no great meaning, only the witness of their own existence, and my having seen them.

We are so afraid of being boring, of not being profound. We want epiphanies, twists, moral lessons. But what about the texture of unremarkable consciousness? The fleeting ache behind the eyes from too much screen time. The taste of slightly stale mint tea. The comfort of a worn cotton shirt. This is the fabric of a life. Not the embroidered crests, but the endless, sturdy weave of the background cloth.

Word count ticking up. The jar is nearly full. I’ve filled it with sand, it seems. Fine, granular moments. A memory of hands, a thought about bridges, the quality of afternoon light. No pebbles of shocking truth, no orchid of stunning beauty. Just sand. But if you look closely at sand, you see each grain is unique, a tiny fragment of a much larger rock, worn down by time and tide, holding the memory of mountain and sea.

So here we are, at the end of the middle. These words are a bridge I built between the silence I started with and the silence you will return to. I walked across it. Now you have, too. And what’s left is not a grand idea, but perhaps a feeling—a resonance, like the hum after a bell is struck. The sound fades into the silence from which it came, but the air is changed, subtly, for having held it.

The last grains of sand trickle into the jar. It is full. Not with answers, but with presence. A testament to a thousand words of being here, in the middle of an ordinary, fleeting, utterly singular afternoon. And that, perhaps, is everything and nothing at all.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
